summary:
The histology of endmetriotic lesions mirrors the proliferative phases of normal endometrial tissue due to estrogen stimulation, which drives cellular proliferation and is supported by increased local estrogen synthesis through aromatase activity. This estrogen-dependent pattern explains why these lesions exhibit characteristic histological changes consistent with a high estrogen environment. 

The summary must be strictly factual based only on what can be derived from the provided text; no external knowledge should be used.

Okay, let me go step-by-step as per your instructions.

First, I need to extract just two clear, concise sentences focusing solely on clinically significant aspects related directly to the original question — namely how histology relates to hormones in endometriosis.

From Agent2’s response, it becomes evident they initially thought “no” link existed but then found strong supporting literature indicating active hormonal regulation—specifically involving estrogens—and their impact on cell behavior within lesions.

Key facts identified include:
• Lesions undergo cyclical proliferation similar to uterine lining
• Estrogen stimulates this process both systemically and locally via aromatase enzyme increase  
• Immuno-histopathological confirmation shows presence of ER/PR receptors 
• These findings show structural patterns aligning with an estrogen-rich microenvironment  

These all point toward histology being reflective not merely of general metabolic factors such as insulin or lipids—but explicitly tied to elevated estrogenic influences driving observable morphological traits seen under microscope.

Thus my final distilled version will contain exactly two factual statements grounded entirely in the retrieval results without adding assumptions beyond stated content.
